situation: When setting up declarative, version-controlled cluster configuration
motivation: So I can replace manual cluster configuration with automated, auditable GitOps workflows
outcome: Cluster configuration managed through Git with full version control and collaboration features
section: Managing OpenShift cluster configuration
evidence: As an administrator, maintaining the stability and consistency of an OpenShift Container Platform environment requires a move away from traditionally manual configurations toward automated, declarative management.
notes: Main workflow covering GitOps adoption for cluster configuration. Module types: assembly (managing-openshift-cluster-configuration.adoc)

situation: When I need to enable GitOps capabilities on my OpenShift cluster
motivation: So I can manage cluster configuration declaratively through Git
outcome: GitOps Operator installed and running in the cluster
section: Installing Red Hat OpenShift GitOps Operator using CLI
evidence: You can install Red Hat OpenShift GitOps Operator from the OperatorHub by using the CLI.
notes: Covers CLI-based operator installation. Module type: PROCEDURE (installing-gitops-operator-using-cli.adoc)

situation: When I need to isolate the GitOps Operator installation
motivation: So I can deploy the operator in a dedicated namespace
outcome: openshift-gitops-operator namespace created
section: Installing Red Hat OpenShift GitOps Operator using CLI
evidence: Create a `openshift-gitops-operator` namespace: $ oc create ns openshift-gitops-operator

job: Enable cluster monitoring for operator namespace
situation: When I need to monitor the GitOps Operator
motivation: So I can track operator health and performance metrics
outcome: Cluster monitoring enabled on the operator namespace
section: Installing Red Hat OpenShift GitOps Operator using CLI
evidence: You can enable cluster monitoring on `openshift-gitops-operator`, or any namespace, by applying the `openshift.io/cluster-monitoring=true` label

situation: When I need to configure operator deployment scope
motivation: So I can define which namespaces the operator can manage
outcome: OperatorGroup created for the GitOps Operator
section: Installing Red Hat OpenShift GitOps Operator using CLI
evidence: Create a `OperatorGroup` object YAML file, for example, `gitops-operator-group.yaml`

situation: When I need to install the operator from OperatorHub
motivation: So I can deploy the GitOps Operator with automatic updates
outcome: Subscription created and operator deployed
section: Installing Red Hat OpenShift GitOps Operator using CLI
evidence: Create a `Subscription` object YAML file to subscribe a namespace to the Red Hat OpenShift GitOps Operator

situation: When I need to confirm the operator installed successfully
motivation: So I can ensure all components are running before proceeding
outcome: All GitOps pods running in both openshift-gitops and operator namespaces
section: Installing Red Hat OpenShift GitOps Operator using CLI
evidence: After the installation is complete, verify that all the pods in the `openshift-gitops` namespace are running

situation: When I need to understand the default instance configuration
motivation: So I can customize it for my cluster configuration needs
outcome: Default Argo CD instance details analyzed and understood
section: Analyzing the default Argo CD instance details
evidence: After installation, you can use the OpenShift Container Platform web console to view the **Operator details** page and analyze the default instance configuration.
notes: Covers inspection of operator-created default instance. Module type: PROCEDURE (analyzing-the-default-instance-details.adoc)

situation: When I need to access operator configuration in the web console
motivation: So I can view the installed GitOps Operator details
outcome: Operator details page displayed
section: Analyzing the default Argo CD instance details
evidence: Click **Installed Operators** under the **Operators** menu, and select the **Red Hat OpenShift GitOps** Operator.

situation: When I need to see all Argo CD instances
motivation: So I can select the default instance for inspection
outcome: Argo CD tab showing all instances
section: Analyzing the default Argo CD instance details
evidence: Select the **Argo CD** tab.

situation: When I need to inspect the openshift-gitops instance
motivation: So I can view its detailed configuration
outcome: Default instance details page displayed
section: Analyzing the default Argo CD instance details
evidence: Click the name of the default instance, `openshift-gitops`, to view its details on a new page.

situation: When I need to analyze the instance specification
motivation: So I can understand how it's configured and identify customization needs
outcome: Instance YAML displayed and analyzed
section: Analyzing the default Argo CD instance details
evidence: Select the **YAML** tab to analyze how it is configured.

situation: When I need to interact with Argo CD through its web interface
motivation: So I can manage applications and verify the instance is accessible
outcome: Successfully logged into Argo CD UI with OpenShift credentials
section: Access the default Argo CD instance
evidence: After analyzing the default Argo CD instance details, you can access it through the Argo CD UI to check whether it is available for use.
notes: Covers accessing the Argo CD web interface. Module type: PROCEDURE (access-the-default-argocd-instance.adoc)

job: Launch Argo CD from application menu
situation: When I need to navigate to the Argo CD UI
motivation: So I can access the cluster's Argo CD instance
outcome: Argo CD login page opened
section: Access the default Argo CD instance
evidence: Click the **Application Launcher** menu in the top right corner of the OpenShift Container Platform web console. Select **Cluster Argo CD** from the dropdown list.

situation: When presented with the Argo CD login page
motivation: So I can authenticate using my existing OpenShift credentials
outcome: Redirected to OpenShift login page
section: Access the default Argo CD instance
evidence: Click the **LOG IN VIA OPENSHIFT** button. The OpenShift Container Platform login page opens.

situation: When I need to provide login credentials
motivation: So I can prove my identity to access Argo CD
outcome: Authentication successful, authorization page displayed
section: Access the default Argo CD instance
evidence: Enter your OpenShift Container Platform credentials. The **Authorize Access** page opens.

situation: When Argo CD requests access to my OpenShift identity
motivation: So Argo CD can verify my permissions and roles
outcome: Permissions granted, Argo CD UI displayed
section: Access the default Argo CD instance
evidence: Click **Allow selected permissions** to provide requested permission. The Argo CD UI page opens.

job: Configure default instance for cluster management
situation: When the default instance has insufficient permissions for production use
motivation: So I can enable the instance to deploy cluster configurations and manage resources
outcome: Default Argo CD instance configured with appropriate RBAC and permissions
section: Configuring the default Argo CD instance
evidence: Though the Operator creates a default Argo CD instance in the `openshift-gitops` namespace, you must configure it to make it useful for deploying applications and setting cluster configuration
notes: Parent section covering both RBAC and permissions configuration. Module type: REFERENCE (configuring-the-default-argocd-instance.adoc)

parent_job: Configure default instance for cluster management
situation: When users need appropriate access to the default Argo CD instance
motivation: So team members can perform necessary tasks in Argo CD while maintaining security
outcome: Users assigned to cluster-admins group with admin role in Argo CD
section: Configuring RBAC
evidence: You must configure RBAC to provide the sufficient access to the users to work with the default instance.
notes: Covers user access configuration. Module type: PROCEDURE (configuring-rbac.adoc)

situation: When I need to see the operator-configured RBAC settings
motivation: So I can understand the current access control policy
outcome: RBAC configuration displayed showing defaultPolicy and group mappings
section: Configuring RBAC
evidence: View the Operator-configured RBAC for the default instance: $ oc get argocd openshift-gitops -n openshift-gitops -o=jsonpath='{.spec.rbac}'

situation: When I need to verify if the required group exists
motivation: So I can determine whether to create or modify the group
outcome: Group existence confirmed
section: Configuring RBAC
evidence: Check if the `cluster-admins` group exists: $ oc get groups

situation: When the cluster-admins group doesn't exist or doesn't include my user
motivation: So I can grant admin access to users in Argo CD
outcome: cluster-admins group created or updated with authorized users
section: Configuring RBAC
evidence: If the group does not exist, create it and add your user to it: $ oc adm groups new cluster-admins <user>

situation: When I need to confirm users are properly assigned to the group
motivation: So I can ensure authorized users have the expected access
outcome: Group membership validated and users must re-login to Argo CD
section: Configuring RBAC
evidence: Validate that the group `cluster-admins` exists and that your user is part of it: $ oc get groups cluster-admins

parent_job: Configure default instance for cluster management
job: Grant cluster-admin permissions to Argo CD
situation: When the default permissions are insufficient for cluster configuration
motivation: So the Argo CD application controller can deploy all necessary cluster resources
outcome: ClusterRoleBinding created granting cluster-admin to the application controller
section: Configuring permissions
evidence: When using the default instance for cluster configuration, provide the `cluster-admin` permissions to the Argo CD application controller service account.
notes: Covers Kubernetes-level permissions. Module type: PROCEDURE (configuring-permissions.adoc)

parent_job: Grant cluster-admin permissions to Argo CD
situation: When I need to elevate permissions for the default instance
motivation: So the application controller can create cluster-scoped resources
outcome: cluster-admin role bound to openshift-gitops-argocd-application-controller service account
section: Configuring permissions
evidence: Run the following command: $ oc adm policy add-cluster-role-to-user --rolebinding-name="openshift-gitops-cluster-admin" cluster-admin -z openshift-gitops-argocd-application-controller -n openshift-gitops

parent_job: Grant cluster-admin permissions to Argo CD
situation: When I need to confirm the permission grant succeeded
motivation: So I can ensure the application controller has the necessary access
outcome: ClusterRoleBinding YAML displayed showing correct role and subject
section: Configuring permissions
evidence: View the created `ClusterRoleBinding` object: $ oc get clusterrolebinding openshift-gitops-cluster-admin -o yaml

situation: When managing cluster configuration through Git repositories
motivation: So I can track changes, enable collaboration, and maintain audit history
outcome: Cluster configuration changes tracked in Git with full history and approval workflow
section: Managing OpenShift cluster configuration
evidence: Version control and auditability: Configuration changes committed to Git provide a complete history of modifications. This facilitates auditing, compliance, and accountability.
notes: Strategic benefit of GitOps approach. Module type: assembly (managing-openshift-cluster-configuration.adoc), Lines 8-9

situation: When cluster configuration changes cause issues
motivation: So I can quickly rollback to a known-good state
outcome: Cluster restored to previous working configuration from Git history
section: Managing OpenShift cluster configuration
evidence: Optimized performance and disaster recovery: GitOps points the Argo CD application to the previous commit or tag with a known-good state in Git, which in turn reduces downtime and helps with disaster recovery.
notes: Recovery workflow enabled by GitOps. Module type: assembly (managing-openshift-cluster-configuration.adoc), Lines 10

situation: When managing complex or multi-cluster environments
motivation: So I can efficiently handle large-scale deployments with minimal manual effort
outcome: Multiple clusters managed consistently through automated GitOps workflows
section: Managing OpenShift cluster configuration
evidence: Efficiency and Scalability: GitOps streamlines the deployment and operations workflows, enabling efficient management of complex and multi-cluster environments with reduced manual intervention and human error.
notes: Scalability benefit of GitOps. Module type: assembly (managing-openshift-cluster-configuration.adoc), Lines 12
